runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Keane and Rogerson (2012) on labor supply elasticities across income distributions
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) elasticity estimates for taxable income
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) survey of labor supply response parameters
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the middle-to-upper-middle earnings distribution (decile 7), substitution elasticity is expected to be modest but non-trivial. This decile typically consists of middle-class workers with some price sensitivity but significant fixed labor supply commitments. Empirical literature generally supports substitution elasticities in the 0.15–0.40 range for this income group, with lower elasticities than secondary earners but higher than top earners. I anchor toward the lower-middle of documented ranges (around 0.25) given decile 7's position in the distribution, behavioral inertia, and relatively less job mobility than lower deciles.

runs.reasoningSummary: For middle-income primary earners (decile 7), substitution elasticities are typically modest. Middle-income earners show less behavioral responsiveness than high earners but more than lower-income earners with binding constraints.
